name: Sonny Rollins
abstract: Walter Theodore "Sonny" Rollins (September 7, 1930 – May 25, 2026) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist who is widely recognized as one of the most important and influential of jazz musicians. In a seven-decade career, Rollins recorded more than 60 albums as a leader.

interviews.title: Sonny Rollins — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jerry Bergonzi
about_quotes.evidence: I said oh goodness. So that’s when I really got the bug. ... And then a friend, when I was twelve a friend of mine came over my house and his father was a trumpet player and he had a Miles record with Coltrane on it, and a Sonny Rollins record...
about_quotes.doc_title: Jerry Bergonzi interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 7, 2022

about_quotes.speaker: Dave Frishberg
about_quotes.evidence: But I never met or got to play with Sonny Rollins. I saw him in person a couple of times, yeah, but I don’t think I ever did have the pleasure of meeting him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave Frishberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 16, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Sheryl Bailey
about_quotes.evidence: I turned it on and I heard Sonny Rollins. And then I heard Charlie Parker. And then I heard Wes Montgomery. And then I just was hooked.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sheryl Bailey interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, July 24, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Ramsey Lewis
about_quotes.evidence: He was just a guy that worked in the shipping department. I never got his name, because they always just called him Sonny.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ramsey Lewis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ron Carter
about_quotes.evidence: We worked together in New York at the old Five Spot for probably six, eight weeks with a trio: saxophone, bass and drums.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ron Carter — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Avakian George
about_quotes.evidence: we became extremely good friends on a level that had nothing to do with music, and we always will be.
about_quotes.doc_title: Avakian George — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
